title: 8.4 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 8.4!

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did World War I change the way people expressed themselves through art and writing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Artists and writers began to express disillusionment, confusion, and a loss of faith in traditional values.
Most people stopped creating art and writing altogether.
Art and literature became more focused on celebrating national pride and military victories.
Writers returned to classical styles and themes to reconnect with the past.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were colonized people especially disillusioned after World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They hoped the war would lead to more freedom, but instead faced continued control.
They believed in communism and were upset it didn’t spread globally after the war.
They were the only group to suffer losses in the war.
They were promised immediate independence by the League of Nations, but it never came.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were Italy and Japan dissatisfied with the Treaty of Versailles?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They felt the treaty did not reward them fairly for their support during the war.
They were forced to pay reparations like Germany.
They were punished by losing their colonies.
They were not permitted to join the League of Nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
During and after World War I, many writers and artists of the Lost Generation rejected the idea that war was noble or heroic. British soldier-poet Wilfred Owen used his own experiences in the trenches to reveal the true horror of modern warfare.

>If in some smothering dreams, you too could pace  
Behind the wagon that we flung him in…  
My friend, you would not tell with such high zest  
To children ardent for some desperate glory,  
The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.  

>—Wilfred Owen, “Dulce et Decorum Est” (1917)  
(Translation of Latin: “It is sweet and fitting to die for one’s country”)


**How does this poem reflect the ideas and feelings of the Lost Generation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It reveals disillusionment with war and rejects the idea that dying for one’s country is noble.
It celebrates bravery and the heroic actions of soldiers in World War I.
It encourages young people to fight for their nation with pride and determination.
It reflects the political tension between Allied and Central Powers during the war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the key difference between a social revolution and a political revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A political revolution changes who holds power, while a social revolution changes deeper structures like class, race, or gender.
Social revolutions are most likely to use the military, while political revolutions remain non-violent.
Social revolutions attempt to change leadership, while political revolutions aim to redistribute the nation’s wealth.
A political revolution’s goal is to end systematic discrimination, while a social revolution’s motive is to end government corruption.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the Mexican Revolution both a political and social revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It overthrew a dictator and aimed to redistribute land and improve workers’ rights.
It removed foreign powers from Mexico and established state-ownership of resources.
It created a new monarchy that addressed long-standing social issues.
It overthrew the Spanish colonial government and granted rights to Indigenous people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the United States respond when revolutionary violence threatened its interests in Mexico?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It sent military forces into Mexico and occupied Veracruz.
It formally annexed northern Mexican territory.
It stayed out of the conflict entirely.
It supported the revolutionary policies of Francisco Madero.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what way did the Mexican Revolution succeed in meeting its goals?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led to a new constitution that guaranteed land and labor rights.
It eliminated all foreign influence in Mexico.
It united all political factions into one lasting party.
It dramatically reduced poverty in rural areas and indigenous communities.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did Russia experience nationalism differently than many of its European neighbors?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Russians did not share a common national identity.
The Tsar punished anyone who expressed nationalist ideas.
The Bolsheviks promoted nationalism, but most Russians rejected it.
Russians were more worried about economic hardships than nationalism.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Provisional Committee eventually lose power to the Bolsheviks in Russia?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Committee kept Russia involved in World War I, which was deeply unpopular.
The Committee failed to fix the food shortages caused by wartime rationing.
The Committee was backed by the Tsar, and people wanted to get rid of anything connected to him.
The people supported the Committee, but the Bolsheviks took power by force.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Bolsheviks change both production and distribution in Russia after taking power?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They established a communist economic system and moved away from private ownership.
The Bolsheviks started a new type of capitalism focused on worker competition.
The Bolsheviks used more resources to continue fighting the First World War.
The Bolsheviks wanted people to work harder for less reward than under the Tsar.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Russian Revolution influence global politics in the 20th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It spread the idea that workers could overthrow powerful governments.
It convinced all major powers to adopt communism.
It helped monarchies regain their former strength.
It made the Soviet Union a minor player in global affairs.
